title: Fired Over Five Dollars
topic: Parents and a Lost Teaching Job
summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Prospect, Pennsylvania, in September 2011, Thomas Walter Gaither introduces his parents, Walter B. Gaither and Fannie Mae Little Gaither, who met at Friendship Junior College and settled in Great Falls, South Carolina, and recounts how his father was fired from teaching for questioning a five-dollar discrepancy a white school-board member was pocketing.

title: A One-Room School at Pleasant Grove
summary: Gaither describes his first seven years of schooling in a one-room school owned by Pleasant Grove AME Zion Church, with a single drum heater that smoked the room, no library beyond his mother's dictionary, hand-me-down textbooks from white children, and the rush of new black elementary schools the South built as the 1954 Supreme Court decision loomed.

title: Fireman and Watchman at the Mill
summary: Gaither recalls church at Pleasant Grove as the center of family life, learning only later that his father's teaching termination was decided by an all-white trustee board with a figurehead black board chosen not to rock the boat, and his father's later work at a cotton mill as a boiler fireman and night watchman before losing that job too.

title: Let's Get Organized
summary: Gaither tells how his father went back to Benedict College for a sociology degree, was denied the local black high school principalship after a Baptist minister opposed him, and rallied the family with the cry let's get organized, loading a 1939 Buick with bricks and mortar to take handyman and masonry jobs that funded the children's path to college.

title: Valedictorian and Salutatorian
topic: A Brick Mason Pays the Way
summary: Gaither describes his father becoming a brick mason who traveled across the Charlotte-Columbia-Orangeburg region and stayed on as master mason through the winter to fund tuition, and how he as valedictorian and his brother as salutatorian won small scholarships, seventy-five and fifty dollars, and chose Claflin College in Orangeburg.

title: The Till Case at the Dinner Table
summary: Resuming after a break, Gaither recalls race openly discussed at the dinner table, his father's horror at the Emmett Till case as a motivating force for his later activism, his father hosting union organizers at the mill, and the family's open support for the NAACP, including a suit to equalize teacher salaries that divided relatives who were teachers.

title: Not a Pauper, Not a Chicken Thief
summary: Gaither recounts his parents being registered voters and his own determination to vote, once driving three hundred miles round trip to cast a ballot, and describes the South Carolina elector's certificate that required interpreting the state constitution and swearing one was not a pauper or convicted of crimes like stealing chickens or wife beating, which he compares to modern voter-ID laws.

part: Claflin College and the Movement's Edge
title: A Same-Class Brother
summary: Gaither explains adjusting academically at Claflin College against students from stronger schools, graduating with honors in general science with a biology concentration, how he and his younger brother ended up in the same graduating class because their mother brought the boy to her school early, and why a black college was the only choice available.

part: Claflin College and the Movement's Edge
title: Eighty-Five Degrees and the GRE
summary: Gaither tells the story of taking the Graduate Record Exam, when about a dozen black students from South Carolina State and Claflin were barred from the University of South Carolina campus and forced into a sweltering eighty-five-degree upstairs room across the street, and how his father from then on refused to root for the university and backed Clemson instead.

title: A Youth Chapter Grows
summary: Gaither recounts becoming president of the Claflin NAACP youth chapter, the small membership that swelled after the sit-ins began, students who apologized for fearing reprisals against their parents, the regional meetings where he met Ruby Hurley and Medgar Evers, and how the Greensboro and Rock Hill sit-ins of February 1960 spurred Orangeburg students to organize.

title: Cracking the Color Line
summary: Gaither describes James T. McCain bringing CORE's organizing tools, the booklet CORE Rules for Action, and James Peck's Cracking the Color Line, students from Claflin and South Carolina State meeting in the Claflin gymnasium to build sociodramas inspired by King's Stride Toward Freedom, and the private-college freedom Claflin had while state-school administrators were chosen to suppress student protest.

title: Idealists and the Fear of Reprisal
summary: Gaither explains that the early sit-in organizing fell mostly to men because women had a seven o'clock dormitory curfew, the mix of idealism and fear of reprisals against parents that drove the students, and why they chose nonviolent direct action over the NAACP's preference for a single test-case arrest and a court ruling.

title: Three Hundred Fifty Arrested
summary: Gaither recounts the mass arrest in Orangeburg, three hundred fifty people taken in until the city and county jails filled and an open-air stockade was used, his own arrest for walking the courthouse square while talking to NAACP youth secretary Herbert Wright, and meeting attorney Matthew J. Perry Jr., who graciously promised the NAACP's resources for their defense.

title: We Have Been Nonviolent Too Long
summary: Gaither describes first hearing Glenn Smiley of the Fellowship of Reconciliation lecture on nonviolence and naively objecting that black people had been nonviolent too long, then coming to embrace it through James T. McCain, whom he calls his civil rights mentor, a low-key tactical practitioner who pointed him toward Gandhi's writings.

title: Ejection of Undesirable Guests
summary: Gaither recounts McCain persuading him to attend CORE's Action Institute in Miami in the summer of 1960, his second arrest at a restaurant in Hollywood, Florida alongside Dottie Miller and Bernard Lafayette, ten or twelve days in the Dade County jail, and the unusual charge brought against them, ejection of undesirable guests.

title: How Would Gandhi Behave
summary: Gaither explains that he came to nonviolence in tactical terms first, and reflects, jumping ahead to the Rock Hill road gang, on weighing how Gandhi would have acted, believing Gandhi would clean the toilets or shovel sand until he collapsed, and on the gradual growth from nonviolence as technique into nonviolence as a way of life he says few people achieve.

title: The Lord Will Take Care of Him
topic: Family Support and a Burned Cross
summary: Gaither recounts his deeply religious family's faith that the Lord would protect him, their own danger in a small community where they were not nonviolent, local men offering to guard his father's house while he was on the Rock Hill road gang, and the cross burned on his parents' lawn that his father vowed would have been the burner's last.

title: A Mostly White National Office
summary: Gaither describes his growing sense of CORE at the national level after visiting its New York office, then mostly white apart from James T. McCain, led by executive secretary James R. Jim Robinson, an organization founded in 1942 at the University of Chicago that championed nonviolent direct action, and his first airplane flight from Columbia to reach it.

title: If My Preacher Says It's Okay
summary: Gaither remembers a summer of voter registration with Frank Robinson and Jim McCain, going door to door at housing projects and finding that resistant people, especially older women, would register and vote only if their preacher gave his blessing, with modest but real success.

title: Willing to Suffer
summary: Gaither reflects that the early sit-ins ended in arrests and posted bonds the state could exploit, leading him to seek a deeper level of commitment, the jail-no-bail policy with the Friendship 9 staged almost exactly a year after the first sit-ins, meant to appeal to people of goodwill through a visible willingness to suffer.

title: The Right Place at the Right Time
summary: Gaither acknowledges an earlier CORE-affiliated jail-no-bail by the Tallahassee group of Patricia and Priscilla Stephens, but argues that historical significance comes from context, that the Friendship 9 acted at a strategic moment after hundreds of students had been arrested following Greensboro, making theirs the protest that did the critical thing.

title: Hot Chocolate in the Cell Block
summary: Gaither explains that the Miami arrest was essentially accidental as observers and test groups sometimes both got taken in, and describes his extended stay in a Dade County cell block of thirty-five men including murderers, the prisoner hierarchy that beat an elderly man into the shower, and inmates melting candy bars by a lamp to make hot chocolate.

title: A Field Secretary on the Road
summary: Gaither recounts his delight at joining CORE full time despite disagreeing with the NAACP's philosophy, his early assignments in Kentucky, California, and Arizona training local people in nonviolent direct action, meeting Medgar Evers as his one contact in Jackson, and being chosen as scout for the Freedom Ride because he had helped originate the idea.

title: Why January 31, Not February 1
summary: Gaither describes training the Friendship College students at Claflin with Gordon Carey on sociodramas, marveling that college students with no knowledge of tactical nonviolence pulled off a major protest, and explaining that the date was set for January 31, 1961 rather than the February 1 anniversary so the students could register for classes and not lose a whole semester to thirty days on the road gang.

title: A Thousand People We Could Not See
topic: On the Rock Hill Road Gang
summary: Gaither reflects on the pivotal jail-no-bail, recalling Clara Luper's Oklahoma City sit-ins that few knew of, and describes life on the Rock Hill road gang where authorities limited visitors to a line of about a hundred while a thousand gathered unseen, a solitary-confinement punishment over building a fence, and learning only later of the impact outside.

title: An Idea on a Bus North
summary: Gaither recounts riding a bus back to New York with Gordon Carey in December 1960, talking over Louis Fischer's book on Gandhi and the 1947 Journey of Reconciliation scouted by George Houser, and how the idea of a Freedom Ride emerged, with him taking on the scout's role of sending back details on towns, bus schedules, and stations and choosing stops near black colleges that could house the riders.

title: If We Get Through Alabama
summary: Gaither explains the audacious plan to push through the Deep South into Mississippi, Alabama, and Louisiana, his belief that federal protection would be needed past Alabama, why SCLC went in where the NAACP was outlawed, the timing as James Farmer became CORE's executive secretary, and how no one estimated the ride would become a signature protest of the movement.

title: King Was Not a Freedom Rider
summary: Gaither says he never expected King to join the ride and holds no reservation about it, crediting King's national advisory role with CORE and his work flushing out nonviolence as strategy, and recounts his own assignment after the Anniston bus burning to Montgomery, where he lived in Ralph Abernathy's home during martial law.

title: A Rap on the Phone Booth
summary: Gaither describes meeting unidentified incoming Freedom Riders at the Montgomery bus stations under martial law, National Guard jeeps escorting them only to within a block, and his signal of rapping on the phone booth as a rider dialed Ralph Abernathy's number, the guard reappearing to return them to Abernathy's black neighborhood before he was assigned to Jackson.

part: After the Ride: Government and COFO
title: Gratification and Responsibility
summary: Gaither, then in his early twenties, reflects on watching his vision unfold amid violence and federal momentum with both gratification and a sense of responsibility for those injured, his resolve that violence must not intimidate the movement, and his view that the federal government and Kennedy administration were not allies, annoyed by black and white riders challenging segregation.

part: After the Ride: Government and COFO
title: Doar Welcomed, the FBI Not
summary: Gaither contrasts the welcomed and sincere Justice Department presence of John Doar in Mississippi voter-registration work with the FBI, which he found could not be trusted in any community he worked, recalling an agent who hoped to implicate him in a conspiracy, and his judgment that to the Kennedys the matter was politics rather than moral commitment.

part: After the Ride: Government and COFO
title: A Cross Section of America
summary: Gaither recalls how the campaign in Jackson and at Parchman Farm drew religious groups and people from across the country, his sense as the summer of 1961 closed that nonviolent direct action could promote change even as impatient voices turned toward violence, and his view that King's star had waned by his assassination as violent elements grew.

part: After the Ride: Government and COFO
title: A Visionary Named Bob Moses
topic: Founding COFO and Working With Moses
summary: Gaither describes being present at the founding of COFO and respecting how each organization had different strengths against a segregationist culture that saw NAACP, CORE, SNCC, and SCLC as all the same, and his high regard for Bob Moses, a visionary who managed local ministers' congressional campaigns and pushed issues rather than himself.

part: From the Draft to Graduate School
title: Not Morally Fit to Serve
summary: Gaither recounts receiving a 1A classification and an induction notice to Fort Jackson while working in Jackson, having missed claiming conscientious-objector status, and how the sergeant, on seeing his arrest record, declared him not morally fit to serve and released him, sending him quickly to resurrect graduate-school offers and enroll at Atlanta University.

part: From the Draft to Graduate School
title: A Law School Letter Arrives Late
summary: Gaither tells how a University of Wisconsin professor named Rice recommended him to law school after he spoke there on the Freedom Rides, and how the admission letter arrived only after he was already halfway through a biology master's degree, so he stayed in biology, won teaching assistantships, and chose the University of Iowa.

part: The Lawyers and a Final Mississippi Summer
title: A Nucleus of Local Lawyers
summary: Gaither honors the little-known black lawyers who tried the Freedom Rider cases in Jackson, Jack H. Young Sr. who studied law by correspondence, R. Jess Brown the former social-studies teacher, and Carsie Hall, the local nucleus reinforced on federal cases by figures like Derrick Bell, Constance Baker Motley, William Kunstler, and Carl Rachlin.

part: The Lawyers and a Final Mississippi Summer
title: A Freedom School in Clarksdale
summary: Gaither describes his final three months in the movement working with Aaron Henry in Clarksdale, Mississippi on a selective-buying boycott of downtown stores, running a freedom school of twenty to twenty-five young people daily near Henry's drugstore, and choosing to stay in Clarksdale over the March on Washington while helping make a sign the local youth carried there.

title: All One-Sided People
summary: Gaither recaps the Council of Federated Organizations as the union of SNCC, SCLC, the NAACP, and CORE that white Mississippi segregationists saw as all one-sided people, then turns to his graduate study at the University of Iowa, where he was the only African American in the department and served as a teaching assistant in a large introductory biology course.

title: Why Iowa, Not Texas
summary: Gaither recounts being compared to a previous African American student from Louisiana, finding enough respect among professors to avoid major problems despite some who merely tolerated him, and explaining that he chose Iowa because the leading scholar in his field had moved to Austin, and he did not want to fight discrimination in its bladed form again in Texas.

title: Threads Under the Electron Microscope
summary: Gaither describes his research in electron microscopy, studying tiny thread-like structures, from Latin for thread, that stagger an organism's release of reproductive structures over time, and using the microscope's superior resolution to answer questions of speciation that a light microscope could not resolve.

title: Not Rookies Who Didn't Know
summary: Gaither argues the Freedom Rides were among the most inclusive and integrative civil rights protests, conceived and executed primarily by CORE, and voices his disappointment with histories that, while crediting the Nashville students who continued the ride after the Anniston bus burning, wrongly cast CORE as rookies rather than originators of an ongoing process.

title: Surface Change, Same Shot-Callers
summary: Gaither, never having lived in the South again, contends the fundamental infrastructure of racism remains under different labels, that black officials can be constrained by the same people who called the shots fifty years ago, points to Katrina and unequal GI benefits, yet affirms his lasting faith that nonviolent tactics can work given a residual moral conscience among many Americans.

title: Surprised by Obama
summary: Gaither recounts his surprise at Barack Obama's election by young people who then failed to persist in defending the change, his hope the nation might reach a more just order, and reflects on the personal cost of pouring his energy into a movement simply to be accepted as a citizen, time a white American would not have had to spend.

title: But for the Grace of God
topic: Great Falls and the Scholar's Loneliness
summary: Gaither closes by picturing Great Falls, South Carolina as a place of men his age idling without hope under a tree, where the South's effort to keep black people down also cheated poor whites, and reflects on the loneliness of being a scholar whose work, a doctorate he frames as a contribution to the whole nation, even his own parents could not fully grasp.
